chunk_summary: Guidelines for initial testing and management of goiter and hypothyroidism in children, emphasizing TSH and free T4 tests, clinical correlation, and avoiding empiric iodine therapy.
learning_objectives: Understand the initial diagnostic approach for pediatric goiter and hypothyroidism, including TSH and free T4 testing.
Recognize clinical signs of hypothyroidism and differentiate between primary hypothyroidism and subclinical cases.
Learn appropriate management strategies for euthyroid goiter and indications for urgent referral.
Identify the role of iodine nutrition and household practices in thyroid health.
key_facts: First-line tests for suspected hypothyroidism are TSH and free T4; high TSH with low free T4 confirms primary hypothyroidism.
Do not treat goiter empirically with iodine drops or thyroid tonics; reinforce iodized salt use instead.
Isolated mild TSH elevation with normal free T4 requires clinical correlation and repeat testing, not immediate high-dose therapy.
Ultrasound is indicated for nodular goiter, asymmetry, rapid enlargement, compressive symptoms, or malignancy suspicion.
Urgent referral is needed for very low free T4, significant symptoms, congenital hypothyroidism, or large goiter with compressive signs.
reference_quote: Do not treat goiter with empiric iodine drops or 'thyroid tonics.' If the child is euthyroid with a small diffuse goiter, reinforce iodized salt use and monitor growth and thyroid function over time. (Indian Academy of Pediatrics Standard Treatment Guidelines: Hypothyroidism)
document_key_topics: Diagnostic approach for pediatric goiter and hypothyroidism
Role of iodine nutrition in thyroid health
Indications for urgent referral in thyroid dysfunction
